items.q: What is the first step in critically engaging with an anthropological source?
items.misconception: Agreeing with the source before reading it fully.
items.why: Students jump to evaluation or summary without first pinning down the argument being made.

items.q: What does evaluating the 'evidence base' involve?
items.correct: Judging whether the fieldwork data adequately supports the claims.
items.misconception: Checking only whether the writing is enjoyable.
items.random_wrong: Measuring the length of the text.
items.why: Learners confuse evaluating evidence with reacting to style rather than assessing support for claims.

items.q: What makes a critical engagement balanced?
items.correct: Considering both the strengths and the limitations of the account.
items.why: Students think criticism means fault-finding, neglecting the strengths side of evaluation.

items.q: Critically engaging with an anthropological source begins with:
items.random_wrong: Copying it out word for word
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Identifying the author's central argument. “Counting the number of pages” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Evaluating the 'evidence base' of an ethnography means assessing:
items.correct: Whether the data supports the claims
items.misconception: The design of the book cover
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Whether the data supports the claims. “The design of the book cover” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Focus critical analysis on a specific aspect of the source
items.why: Correct answer: Focus critical analysis on a specific aspect of the source. “Summarise the whole of anthropology” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A strong critical engagement considers an account's:
items.why: It's Strengths and limitations. “Strengths only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
